content.text: Dynamic tool loading means choosing which tools to include in each API call based on the current conversation state, rather than sending every available tool every time. The model's accuracy at selecting the right tool degrades as the tool list grows. Keeping each turn's tool set small and relevant — 5 to 15 tools — produces faster, more focused responses than exposing a catalog of 100 tools all at once.

attrs.alt: Dynamic tool loading: per-turn tool selection based on conversation intent
attrs.title: Dynamic Tool Loading Per-Turn

content.text: Every tool you add to the tools= list costs attention. The model must read every description on every turn. Sending 80 tools when 8 are relevant is not safety — it is noise.
A customer support agent might have 60 tools across triage, billing, shipping, escalation, and account management. Sending all 60 on every turn has concrete costs:
Tool descriptions are part of the system prompt counted toward input tokens. 60 verbose descriptions can add 3,000–8,000 tokens per turn.
Research and empirical observation both show models make more selection errors as the number of available tools increases beyond roughly 20.
The model must process every description before generating a response; a smaller list generates first tokens faster.
The simplest dynamic strategy classifies the conversation intent after each model turn and selects a matching tool group for the next call.

Keyword matching is intentionally cheap: it runs between turns with no extra API call. A more accurate alternative is a second, lightweight API call that classifies intent from the conversation so far — worth the cost if misclassification is expensive.
inspects the assistant's text and may shift
then loads the billing or escalation group. The model never sees all 60 tools at once.

When keyword-based groups are too coarse, embedding-based search selects the most relevant tools for the user's query. Each tool description is pre-embedded; at runtime the query is embedded and the closest tools by cosine similarity are retrieved.

import numpy as np
from anthropic import Anthropic
from mytools.registry import ToolDef, ToolRegistry

client = Anthropic()

def embed(text: str) -> list[float]:
    """Embed a string using the Anthropic (or any) embeddings API."""
    # Replace with your embedding provider:
    resp = client.embeddings.create(model="voyage-3", input=[text])
    return resp.data[0].embedding

class SemanticToolSelector:
    def __init__(self, registry: ToolRegistry) -> None:
        self._registry = registry
        self._index: list[tuple[ToolDef, list[float]]] = []

    def build_index(self) -> None:
        """Pre-embed all tool descriptions. Call once at startup."""
        for tool in self._registry.list_all():
            text = f"{tool.name}: {tool.description}"
            self._index.append((tool, embed(text)))

    def top_k(self, query: str, k: int = 10) -> list[ToolDef]:
        """Return the k most relevant tools for this query."""
        q_vec = np.array(embed(query))
        scored = []
        for tool, vec in self._index:
            score = float(np.dot(q_vec, np.array(vec)))
            scored.append((score, tool))
        scored.sort(key=lambda x: -x[0])
        return [tool for _, tool in scored[:k]]

Building the index once at startup embeds all tool descriptions. Each query embeds the user's message and retrieves the top-k most similar tools in O(n) time — fast enough for a catalog of hundreds of tools. For very large catalogs, use a vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector) to replace the linear scan.

Some tools should always be available regardless of intent: a clarification tool, a "hand off to human" tool, or a no-op tool the model can call when it is unsure what to do. Add them as a fixed baseline on top of the dynamically selected set.

in the billing intent group, the model might be unable to escalate even if billing tools cannot resolve the issue. Always-on tools provide graceful escape paths regardless of which intent group is active.
Multi-domain agents with clear intent signals
Second API call to small model
High-value flows where misrouting is costly
Large catalogs (50+ tools) with broad scope

import pytest
from mytools.registry import ToolRegistry, ToolDef
from mytools.agent import select_tools, detect_intent

@pytest.fixture
def reg():
    r = ToolRegistry()
    for name, tags in [
        ("search_kb",         ["read", "kb"]),
        ("get_customer",      ["read", "crm"]),
        ("get_invoice",       ["billing"]),
        ("create_refund",     ["billing"]),
        ("create_ticket",     ["escalation"]),
        ("notify_manager",    ["escalation"]),
    ]:
        r.register(ToolDef(
            name=name, description=name,
            input_schema={"type":"object","properties":{}},
            handler=lambda **_: {},
            tags=tags,
        ))
    return r

def test_triage_excludes_billing(reg):
    tools = select_tools(reg, "triage")
    names = {t["name"] for t in tools}
    assert "get_invoice" not in names
    assert "search_kb" in names

def test_billing_excludes_escalation(reg):
    tools = select_tools(reg, "billing")
    names = {t["name"] for t in tools}
    assert "create_refund" in names
    assert "create_ticket" not in names

def test_detect_intent_billing():
    assert detect_intent("I see you have an unpaid invoice.") == "billing"

def test_detect_intent_default():
    assert detect_intent("How can I help you today?") == "triage"

In long conversations the intent can shift multiple times before the model needs a new tool. Recomputing the tool set on every assistant turn wastes CPU and can cause jarring tool set flips mid-task. A simple debounce only recomputes when the detected intent changes.

class IntentTracker:
    def __init__(self, debounce: int = 2) -> None:
        self._current: str = "triage"
        self._candidate: str = "triage"
        self._candidate_count: int = 0
        self._debounce = debounce   # require N consecutive same-intent signals

    def update(self, detected: str) -> str:
        """Return the (possibly unchanged) current intent."""
        if detected == self._current:
            self._candidate = detected
            self._candidate_count = 0
            return self._current
        if detected == self._candidate:
            self._candidate_count += 1
            if self._candidate_count >= self._debounce:
                self._current = detected
                self._candidate_count = 0
        else:
            self._candidate = detected
            self._candidate_count = 1
        return self._current

# In the agent loop:
# tracker = IntentTracker(debounce=2)
# ...
# intent = tracker.update(detect_intent(assistant_text))
# active_tools = select_tools(registry, intent)
, the intent must appear twice consecutively before the tool set switches. A single assistant turn mentioning "invoice" does not immediately swap all billing tools in — the signal must repeat.

When debugging why the model called the wrong tool (or failed to call any tool), the first question is: was the right tool in the tools= list for that turn? Log the tool names sent on each call so the answer is trivially available.

import logging

_LOG = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def select_and_log(
    registry,
    intent: str,
    turn_number: int,
) -> list[dict]:
    tools = select_tools_with_baseline(registry, intent)
    _LOG.debug(
        "turn=%d intent=%s tools=[%s]",
        turn_number,
        intent,
        ", ".join(t["name"] for t in tools),
    )
    return tools
level this log line is cheap and filtered out in production. In a staging environment or when investigating an incident, set the logger to
and every turn's tool list is visible in the output.

The model may occasionally call a tool name that is not in the active set — either because the description of an always-on tool hinted at a capability outside the current group, or because an intent shift mid-turn loaded different tools than expected. Handle this gracefully rather than raising an exception.

The error message is intentionally model-readable: it tells the model that the tool is unavailable in the current context and suggests restating the request. This lets the conversation recover without surfacing an internal error to the user.
Send only the tools relevant to the current conversation turn — not the full catalog. Fewer tools produce faster, more accurate selection.
Use intent groups (keyword detection on assistant text) as a lightweight per-turn filter when the domain has clear sub-domains.
Embed tool descriptions at startup and use cosine similarity to select the top-k tools for each user query when the catalog is large or breadth is unpredictable.
Always include a fixed baseline of escape-hatch tools (escalate, clarify, end conversation) regardless of which intent group is active.
Test that each intent group contains the right tools and explicitly excludes the wrong ones.
